question.text: Scenario: Bharati Infosystems Ltd. (BIL), a listed IT company, has 2 crore outstanding equity shares with a face value of Rs.5 each. The current market price is Rs.400 per share. BIL has accumulated free reserves of Rs.500 crore and paid-up capital of Rs.10 crore. On March 1, 2025, the Board of Directors approves a bonus issue in the ratio of 1:1 (one bonus share for every one share held). The record date is set as March 15, 2025 (T Day). Naveen Sharma, the RTA officer, is responsible for processing the bonus issue. The beneficial owner download from the depository shows 15,000 individual shareholders and 200 institutional shareholders. Three months later, on June 15, 2025, BIL's Board announces a buyback of shares through tender offer at Rs.220 per share (post-bonus adjusted price), for a total buyback amount of Rs.88 crore. The record date for the buyback is July 1, 2025. Ramesh Gupta, a retail investor, holds 2,000 shares (post-bonus) with a market value of Rs.4.2 lakh on the record date. Seema Jain, another investor, holds 800 post-bonus shares with a market value of Rs.1.68 lakh on the record date. Question 4 of 5: For the buyback announced by BIL at Rs.88 crore, does the company need a special resolution from shareholders?

answer.text: Yes, because the buyback amount of Rs.88 crore exceeds 10% of the post-bonus total of paid-up capital and free reserves, necessitating a special resolution
Yes, because all buybacks mandatorily require a special resolution under SEBI regulations
No, because buyback through tender offer only needs Board approval regardless of amount
No, because the buyback amount is within 25% of paid-up capital and free reserves

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Post-bonus paid-up capital = Rs.20 crore (4 crore shares × Rs.5). Free reserves after bonus capitalization ≈ Rs.490 crore (Rs.500 crore minus Rs.10 crore transferred to share capital). Total = Rs.510 crore. 10% threshold = Rs.51 crore. Since Rs.88 crore > Rs.51 crore, a Board resolution alone is insufficient -- a special resolution is required. The buyback is within the 25% maximum limit (Rs.127.5 crore). **The Trap:** Candidates may forget that the bonus issue changes the paid-up capital and reserves figures. Also, being within 25% does NOT mean Board resolution suffices -- the 10% threshold determines whether special resolution is needed. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX covers buyback resolution requirements in detail; Companies Act 2013 Section 68 specifies these thresholds. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When a company announces a buyback requiring a special resolution, monitor the EGM proceedings. If the resolution is not passed, the buyback cannot proceed. Inform clients about the voting timeline.
